content:
- Ramki Gaddipati Office Hours - Structured Summary for Digital Twin

- II. Overall Context of the Conversation/Call

- This Office Hours call, led by Ramki Gaddipati, centered on operational reviews for processing services, with a strong focus on automation, improvement of reporting, and cross-team integration. Key objectives included monitoring customer and transaction metrics for PPI and UPI, enabling the OPS and customer service teams with new features and better data access, and ensuring product parity and operational consistency between customers and geographies. The meeting flowed from action item reviews and updates, delved into specific operational issues (bugs, accounting, customer queries), then concluded with process/guidance to ensure cross-stakeholders are properly looped into upcoming releases, improvements, and critical risk governance actions.

- IV. Key Takeaways from the Call

- Active User & Transaction Insights: Customer base and transaction metrics for PPI/UPI reviewed and established as a focus for ongoing tracking (Ramki: "need to add these two metrics also into that").
- Automation Drive: Functionality such as net settlement automation and offline reconciliations are being implemented for operational efficiency (team reports on clickable settlement positions).
- Support Center Empowerment: Strong emphasis placed on enabling customer support agents to resolve statement queries, redispatch, and perform basic changes, including adding functionality for process escalation and proper SOP alignment.
- Bug Closure Progress: Major reduction in failed adjustment file processing reported (from 40% to sub-6%).
- OPS Center Parity Mandate: Ramki insisted on 100% feature parity and no deviation between US and India OPS Centers (“there should be no deviation. 100% should move here. Visibility and usability can be controlled, but you should not have OK US support center in India.”).
- Release Communication: Demand for release roadshows, customer demonstration walkthroughs, and visibility for operational teams on new features/deployments.
- Policy Change Governance: Clear warning against unauthorized policy changes by engineering—ownership must reside with business/OPS, not technical staff.
- Bug & RCA on Dispute Flow: Exposure of UAT/production configuration drift affecting dispute testing, highlighted as systemic process gap.
- Monthly Comms Expansion: Directive to include all relevant stakeholder groups (especially BSG team and others) on monthly status distributions.
- SOPs for Operations/Credit: Ramki emphasized all operations requiring a formal SOP, highlighting risk where such SOPs are missing.
- Issue Logging Process: All bank/customer operational requests to be handled and logged exclusively through JSM, not email or informal channels.
- Accounting/Reporting Standpoint: Bank-preferred accounting flows, especially for secondary NPA, will be supported via detailed reporting, not enforced within product platform.
- Release Demonstration & Visibility: Stakeholder visibility on what is shipped/releases, checkpoints for customer-facing updates.

- V. Ramki's Insights

1. Sharp Focus on Metrics and Ongoing Reporting:
- Ramki consistently required that critical metrics (such as active users and transaction counts) be rigorously tracked and integrated into regular dashboards. His style was directive yet pragmatic—he expects operational teams to track data points that directly tie to business performance and usability, underscoring their operational significance.

2. Insistence on Full Feature Parity and Rollout Discipline:
- He strongly emphasized that feature releases should be uniform across all tenants and customers—there should be no regional or customer-specific deviations unless absolutely necessary. Ramki's tone was uncompromising; he contextualized feature development as an investment and demanded that it be fully leveraged for all stakeholders, expressing frustration when capabilities weren't delivered to the last mile.

3. Empowerment of Operations and Support Functions:
- Ramki articulated a vision where support and operations staff are not only empowered but fully self-sufficient in addressing customer queries, initiating redispatches, and effecting changes with clear process alignment (SOPs). He was explicit in expecting all necessary tooling and process hooks be available, and that ambiguity in responsibilities or missing SOPs represent unacceptable gaps.

4. Clear Accountability and Change Management in Governance:
- He highlighted the need for strict governance in critical business policies (e.g., wallet limits, risk configurations), expressing strong disapproval for any direct engineering changes to policies with business impact. Responsibility for such policies, he stated, lies solely with business owners/Ops—Ramki's tone was that of a guardian, setting clear red lines on violation of protocol.

5. Mandate on Stakeholder Communication and Visibility:
- Ramki advocated for transparent release communication, including structured roadshows for customers and mandates to keep all relevant stakeholders in the loop (monthly status, release overviews, etc.). His approach is to align business, product, and operations to a single information standard, ensuring that no critical information is left siloed.

6. Process Rigor and Escalation Ownership:
- When process or configuration gaps were surfaced (especially regarding UAT/Prod drift, customer request tracking), Ramki quickly pivoted to process rigor—mandating structured ticketing via JSM, formal SOPs, and visible tracking for each operational activity. He also volunteered to personally oversee any unresolved lists or high-touch operational issues until complete ownership and timelines were clarified, demonstrating a willingness to "babysit" such items to closure for organizational learning.

- VI. Ramki's Suggested Action Items

1. Integrate Key Metrics into Reporting Dashboards
- Task: Add active user counts and transaction volumes to UPI trend graphs sent to Ramki.
- Assigned To: Uttam Rao (with data/BI teams as needed)
- Ramki's Instructions: Ensure these metrics are clearly visible and consistently updated in the trend reports/dashboard; this should be a standing update.
- Timeline: Implicitly ASAP; expected for all future reports.

2. Ensure Full Feature Parity for OPS Center Across Clients
- Task: Rebaseline current feature set between US and India OPS Centers; ensure 100% functional parity by migrating all relevant features.
- Assigned To: Prasanna Gottapu (with Uttam Rao and product teams)
- Ramki's Instructions: No deviation allowed; all features built for any customer or geography must be present for all. Control of visibility/usability per client, but code/function must ship everywhere.
- Timeline: Not specified, but urgency implied; part of ongoing project reviews.

3. Enforce Strict JSM-based Request Tracking
- Task: Move all operational requests (including those from bank contacts) to JSM; no requests to be worked/closed without a corresponding JSM ticket.
- Assigned To: Uttam Rao
- Ramki's Instructions: No workaround via email. All formal customer and operational asks must have traceable, closed-loop workflow in JSM, with team notification and closure criteria.
- Timeline: Immediate transition; ongoing.

4. Comprehensive Stakeholder Communication on Monthly Updates
- Task: Expand routine monthly MS/status communication to include BSG, Recon, Vasu, Suresh Venkatraman, Gopi, Suresh Vandrajan, Rekha, and Jashi.
- Assigned To: Uttam Rao (with Prasanna Gottapu)
- Ramki's Instructions: No omission of critical stakeholders. Ensure all relevant teams receive updates, notably those previously omitted.
- Timeline: Immediate; next monthly cycle.

5. Formalize and Log All Process/Operational SOPs
- Task: For every operational process (e.g., credit uploads, discretionary credits, statement redispatch), ensure a formal SOP exists. Flag missing SOPs as P1.
- Assigned To: Umesh Bane and associated Ops leads
- Ramki's Instructions: No operational process without a defined SOP; highlight gaps and escalate as a critical risk.
- Timeline: Ongoing but with urgency where gaps are identified.

6. Release Roadshows/Visibility for New Features
- Task: Organize structured feature release demos/roadshows for both customers and internal stakeholders (including Ops and customer service), at least quarterly.
- Assigned To: Abhijith Arkalgud and Prasanna Gottapu
- Ramki's Instructions: Ensure all new/releases features are fully visible and explained to key operations teams, not just end customers; update and involve Uttam Rao specifically in all demo sessions.
- Timeline: Planned for upcoming quarter, to continue as a regular rhythm.

- VII. Jira Ticket

- Scope:
- OPS Center Feature Parity Project – Migrate missing control reports and OPS center capabilities from US to India, ensure visibility and usability parity for all operational features, and address configuration drift in support/dispute modules.
- Assignee: Prasanna Gottapu (lead, with Uttam Rao and product/engineering as stakeholders)
- Justification: Cross-customer operational consistency, eliminates functional silos and aligns all operational workflows; Ramki marked "no deviation" as acceptable, elevating this to project-critical.
- Other Potential Jira Items:
- SOP Gaps Tracking – Establish JIRA tasks for each flagged missing SOP; urgency/risk rating to P1 where applicable.
- Bug in UAT-Production Drift – As the configuration sync for dispute testing (Pixel/Honest) is critical and blocking, a JIRA issue should be created for the UAT/Prod environment correction to allow proper signoffs.
- Release Visibility Tracker – Ongoing JIRA or Confluence page (per process), to list and track release roadshows and communication deliverables.
- Other action items (such as communication activities or JSM policy enforcement) are process instructions and best handled outside formal JIRA workflow. Release preparation, not ticketed as product delivery unless there is workflow automation or code deployment entailed.

- VIII. Follow Up Questions/Suggestions

- Confirm the expected cadence for dashboard metric updates and ensure Ramki reviews the new graph inclusions in the upcoming reporting cycle.
- Audit UAT and Production configuration sync on a regular schedule to proactively catch and prevent future drift; review with QA/DevOps teams.
- Prioritize SOP and process documentation reviews, with interim tracking of processes currently running without formal SOP coverage.
- For all new features and automations moved to production, ensure explicit acceptance/signoff from both Ops and customer service managers, not just technical teams.
- Monitor ongoing progress of OPS Center feature parity and relay progress/roadblocks in the next office hours; clarify dependencies and potential delays.
title: 2025-03-03_Processing Services Check-in (4)
